title: Polyscias excelsa
intro: Polyscias excelsa, also known as the 'Mamala Tree' or 'Pacific Elm', is a species of flowering plant in the family Araliaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in Pacific Island medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Bioactive constituents of Polyscias excelsa include flavonoids, terpenoids, and saponins, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Polyscias excelsa is commonly prepared as a tea or a tincture to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, coughs, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Polyscias excelsa is classified in the family Araliaceae, a group of plants commonly known as the ginseng or ivy family. This classification is based on the plant's shared characteristics, such as its leaf structure and reproductive patterns. The genus Polyscias is part of the Araliaceae family, which includes other species of trees and shrubs. The family Araliaceae is further classified in the order Apiales, which comprises a diverse range of plants. As part of the Araliaceae family, Polyscias excelsa shares common ancestors and evolutionary traits with other species in this family.
uses: Polyscias excelsa, a plant native to the Pacific Islands, is valued for its medicinal properties. parts: Polyscias excelsa, also known as the Tī Kouka or Cabbage Tree, is a plant native to New Zealand. The leaves and bark of Polyscias excelsa have been traditionally used in Maori medicine to treat various ailments. The plant's leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. In traditional Maori medicine, the leaves are often crushed and applied topically to treat skin conditions and wounds. The bark of the plant is also used to treat fever and other respiratory issues.
